title: Ranunculus quadrivaginatus
intro: Ranunculus quadrivaginatus, also known as the wandering cudweed, is a perennial plant species belonging to the buttercup family (Ranunculaceae). The plant is valued for its potential health benefits, including its anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties, which may aid in reducing pain and inflammation, as well as protecting against oxidative stress and cell damage. The bioactive constituents of R. quadrivaginatus include sesquiterpene lactones, flavonoids, and phenolic acids, which contribute to its medicinal properties. In traditional herbal medicine, R. quadrivaginatus is often used to make herbal teas, infusions, and tinctures, which are used to treat various ailments, including digestive issues and skin conditions.
taxonomy: Ranunculus quadrivaginatus is classified in the kingdom Plantae, further categorized into the clade Angiosperms, and then into the order Ranunculales. It belongs to the family Ranunculaceae, commonly known as the buttercup family, and is a part of the genus Ranunculus. This classification is based on its characteristics, such as its leaf and flower structure, and its evolutionary relationships with other plants. Ranunculus quadrivaginatus is a species of flowering plant native to the mountainous regions of Asia. Its classification is a reflection of its botanical identity and its place within the plant kingdom.

properties: Ranunculus quadrivaginatus, also known as the wandering dodder, is a plant that is used in traditional medicine in some parts of the world.
